cards.front: What are the two main types of scope in C++?
What are the two main types of duration in C++?
cards.back: Automatic duration (created/destroyed with scope) and static duration (exists for program lifetime).

cards.front: What are the three types of linkage in C++?
cards.back:
• No linkage (local variables)
  • Internal linkage (visible in one file only)
  • External linkage (visible across files)

cards.text: Local variables have {{c1::block scope}}, {{c2::automatic duration}}, and {{c3::no linkage}} by default.
Global variables have {{c1::file scope}}, {{c2::static duration}}, and {{c3::external linkage}} by default (if non-const).
Const global variables have {{c1::file scope}}, {{c2::static duration}}, and {{c3::internal linkage}} by default.
Static local variables have {{c1::block scope}}, {{c2::static duration}}, and {{c3::no linkage}}.

cards.front: What are the default scope, duration, and linkage for functions?
cards.back: Functions have file scope, static duration, and external linkage by default.

cards.question: What are the scope, duration, and linkage of this variable?
cards.explanation: Static local variables have block scope (only accessible in the function), static duration (persist for program lifetime), and no linkage (not accessible from other files).

cards.question: What are the scope, duration, and linkage of this variable?
cards.explanation: Const global variables have file scope, static duration, and internal linkage by default (different from non-const globals which have external linkage).

cards.front: How do inline variables (C++17) affect linkage?
cards.back: Inline variables can have external linkage even when const. They allow a single variable definition to be safely included in multiple translation units.

cards.concept: Key decision tree for variable properties
cards.explanation:
When choosing how to declare a variable, consider:
  • Scope: Where does it need to be accessed? (narrowest possible)
  • Duration: How long should it live? (shortest necessary)
  • Linkage: Should other files see it? (minimize exposure)
Prefer local variables unless you have a specific reason for global scope.
examples: // Local: narrow scope, auto duration, no linkage
// Global const: file scope, static duration, internal
// Shared const: file scope, static duration, external

cards.text: The two main types of scope are {{c1::block scope}} (local) and {{c2::file scope}} (global).
The two main types of duration are {{c1::automatic}} (created/destroyed with scope) and {{c2::static}} (program lifetime).
Local variables have {{c1::no linkage}}, static globals have {{c2::internal linkage}}, and non-const globals have {{c3::external linkage}} by default.
